description: Input or demand 'pushali' a person or system when it exceeds the present capacity needed to receive, process, or act. Several alarms may overload a listener before they overload the signalling system. Capacity changes with the person or system and its circumstances. Time, support, and pain may all alter it; 'ritako' instead records expected work across an interval.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into high rounded back 'u'. Raise the tongue blade behind the alveolar ridge for stressed unvoiced 'sh', pass air along the groove, and open into central 'a'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l', let air pass along its sides, and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Deep 'pu' opens into the wide hush of stressed 'sha', then narrows sharply in bright 'li'. The contour fills the middle of the mouth before finding a smaller way out.
usage_notes: The verb is transitive: the input or demand is the subject and the affected person or system is the object. Its event noun is overload. The surrounding account may name the capacity exceeded, the conditions, and what became harder afterwards.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Attention changes with pain, fatigue, fear, noise, and practice; overload names a condition of the moment rather than a failed character.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A speaker can ask for less input or another pace before inability is mistaken for refusal, indifference, or lack of thought.

semantic_domains.activity: demand exceeding the capacity needed to continue an action
semantic_domains.cognition: information or sensory input exceeding current processing capacity
semantic_domains.physical: bodily burden produced by excessive input or demand
